title: Generalized measurement incompatibility
authors: Edwin Peter Lobo, Maria Balanzó-Juandó, Stefano Pironio
abstract: Quantum measurements can be incompatible, i.e., they can fail to be jointly measurable. Recently, a weaker notion of joint-measurability, called partial joint-measurability, was proposed by Masini et al. in [Quantum 8, 1574 (2024)]. In this work, we further generalize this notion to the setting where only a subset of the outcomes of each measurement is required to be jointly determined by classical variables. We provide two mathematical formulations of partial joint-measurability and show that, like full joint-measurability, it can be decided by solving a single semidefinite program. We prove that in the case of an untrusted measurement device, an adversary Eve, limited to classical side information, can perfectly guess the outcomes of the measurement device if and only if the set of measurements is partially jointly measurable. We derive analytical thresholds on the detection efficiency below which generic measurements become partially jointly measurable. Such bounds directly yield limits on the robustness of device-independent and semi-device-independent quantum cryptographic protocols against detection inefficiency. In particular, our results highlight the importance of a careful treatment of postselection in security analyses.
summary: This work generalizes joint-measurability to partial joint-measurability (G-JM) for quantum measurements, providing necessary and sufficient mathematical conditions for this property. The authors derive quantitative bounds on detection efficiency ($\eta$) that determine when measurements are G-JM, which in turn sets fundamental limits on the security of device-independent quantum key distribution protocols.
main_problem: The paper generalizes the concept of joint-measurability to 'partial joint-measurability' (G-JM) for quantum measurements, particularly in the context of untrusted measurement devices.
main_result: It provides mathematical formulations for G-JM, showing it is decidable by an SDP, and derives analytical thresholds on detection efficiency ($\eta$) that limit the robustness of device-independent quantum cryptographic protocols.
method: The authors use mathematical formulations involving quantum instruments and POVMs, deriving bounds by optimizing over various parameters related to measurement settings and detection efficiency.
model_or_system: The model involves quantum measurements acting on a Hilbert space, characterized by POVMs $\{B_{b|y}\}_{b}$ indexed by measurement settings $y$. The analysis extends to lossy photonic implementations and qubit observables.
key_observables: Detection efficiency ($\eta$), the angular aperture ($	heta$) of the measurement axes, and the conditional Shannon entropies $H(A|E)$ vs $H(A|E')$.
important_parameters:
Detection efficiency ($\eta$), number of measurements ($n$), and the visibility ($
u$) of the measurement device.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes an adversary Eve is limited to classical side information, and the results are derived for generic strategies independent of the specific POVM form.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the simulation process of measurements, the angular aperture $	heta$ of the measurement axes, and the maximum detection efficiency $\eta^*$ as a function of $	heta$ for different types of partial joint-measurability.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first defining G-JM, providing multiple mathematical formulations, and then applying these concepts to derive quantitative bounds on $\eta$ for various partial joint-measurability cases (input, outcome, and combined). The structure culminates in applying these bounds to analyze the security of DIQKD protocols.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical framework for characterizing measurement compatibility, especially when accounting for realistic imperfections like detection loss, directly impacts the theoretical limits of quantum communication protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Left: The overall simulation of the measurements By consists of first applying the instrument {Ic}c to ρ, obtaining c and Ic(ρ), and then performing the measure- ment My,c on Ic(ρ) to obtain b. Right: The combination of the quantum instrument I = {Ic}c and the measure- ment My,c = {Mb|y,c}b can be seen as a single effective POVM Ey = {Ec,b|y}c,b with input y and outcomes (c, b).

FIG. 2: Illustration of the angle θ defined in Eq. (21). It is the angular aperture of the double cone that contains all the measurement axes while having the smallest aper- ture. This figure shows five measurement axes {±⃗ry}5 y=1 (blue), and the axis ⃗m (red) of the corresponding double cone.

FIG. 3: Illustration of the angle θ defined in Eq. (38). It is the largest angle between the first measurement axis {±⃗r1} (red) and the remaining measurement axes {±⃗ry}n y=2 (blue), shown here for n = 7. Equivalently, 2θ is the angular aperture of the double cone with axis ⃗r1 that contains all the measurement axes while having the smallest aperture.
FIG. 4: The maximum detection efficiency η∗for which the lossy measurements {Z, cos(θ)Z +sin(θ)X} are G-JM at different values of visibility ν.
